title: Lastrea canariensis
intro: Lastrea canariensis, also known as Canary Island Lady fern, is a species of fern native to the Canary Islands. This plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial effects, which may help to soothe digestive issues and reduce inflammation. The bioactive constituents of Lastrea canariensis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Lastrea canariensis is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to support digestive health and alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).
taxonomy: Lastrea canariensis is a species of fern in the family Athyriaceae. It is native to the Canary Islands. The genus Lastrea is closely related to other fern genera such as Diplazium and Athyrium. This species was previously classified as Diplazium canariense. Further research is needed to confirm its exact classification within the Athyriaceae family.

parts: Lastrea canariensis is known as Canary Islands polypody or Spanish shield fern. The medicinal parts of Lastrea canariensis are the fronds. They are used in traditional medicine for their anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The plant is traditionally used to treat various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The fronds are also used to make tea, which is believed to have various health benefits.
preparations: Lastrea canariensis is primarily used in herbal preparations for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. The leaves of the plant are harvested and used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, including digestive issues and skin problems. In some cultures, Lastrea canariensis is used to prepare teas and infusions to promote relaxation and reduce stress. The plant's extracts are also used in topical applications to soothe skin irritations and wounds. However, its effectiveness and safety as a medicinal plant require further research and validation.
